target.name: parvistemoline (1)
target.motivation: The parvistemoline family of Stemona alkaloids are structurally unique, featuring a fused tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold with densely contiguous tertiary stereocenters. Their chemical synthesis has remained elusive, with only one prior report outlining synthetic efforts towards a simplified core. The functionalized polycycle with up to seven, densely contiguous tertiary stereocenters is a formidable challenge.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: asymmetric Ir/amine-catalyzed allylation towards a simplified tricyclic core

success_criteria.free_text: Catalytic, asymmetric total syntheses in 13–14 steps from commercially available 2-(methoxycarbonyl)-pyrrole, featuring stereocontrolled Ir/Pd-catalyzed allylic alkylation.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond formation between the pyrrole core and the γ-butyrolactone
key_disconnections.reasoning: To install the monocyclic lactone for parvistemonine (2).

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond formation between the pyrrole core and the γ-butyrolactone
key_disconnections.reasoning: To install the monocyclic lactone for didehydroparvistemonine (3).

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond formation to form the tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold
key_disconnections.reasoning: To deliver the fused tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond formation to form the seven-membered ring
C-C bond formation at the α-position of a keto ester
key_disconnections.reasoning: To install two contiguous tertiary stereocenters with high stereocontrol, stereodivergently accessible to four stereoisomers.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis began with a key Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation to establish two contiguous tertiary stereocenters. Subsequent diastereoselective reduction of the ketone and Krische’s Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation installed other adjacent stereogenic centers. An oxy-Michael addition formed the fused tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold. Later stages involved hydrogenation or oxidation of the pyrrole moiety and vinylogous Mannich reaction or Krische’s allylation to complete the target alkaloids.

thought.background: The authors aimed to develop a stereoselective allylic alkylation of β-keto esters using an Ir/Pd dual catalysis system, inspired by Tanaka and Kitamura's work with Pd/Ru catalysis. They adopted Carreira’s Ir/(phosphoramidite, olefin) catalyst system for Ir-catalyzed allylation.
thought.gap: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation of α-non-substituted keto esters with secondary aryl-substituted alcohols needed to be determined.
thought.decision: To test the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation, p-methyl-α-vinylbenzyl alcohol (10a) was chosen as the model substrate to react with tert-butyl 3-oxobutanoate (11) under standard Ir-catalyzed allylation conditions, with the addition of a Pd catalyst.

outcome.observation: Allylated β-keto ester 12a was obtained in 86% yield with 20:1 dr and >99% ee.

evidence.text_span: As shown in Table 1, treatment of the above electrophilic Ir π-allyl complex with the addition of tert-butyl 3-oxobutanoate (11) and Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 in THF at 50 °C for 1 h was found to be optimal. Under these conditions, allylated β-keto ester 12a was obtained in 86% yield with 20:1 dr and >99% ee (entry 1).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using THF as solvent.
thought.gap: The effect of changing the solvent on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the solvent effect, the reaction was performed in toluene instead of THF.

outcome.observation: Changing the solvent to toluene resulted in a decreased yield (59%) while maintaining excellent selectivities.

evidence.text_span: Changing the solvent to toluene, the reaction was performed with decreased yield but still with excellent selectivities (entry 2).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established at 50 °C.
thought.gap: The effect of lowering the reaction temperature on the yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the temperature effect, the reaction was performed at room temperature instead of 50 °C.

outcome.observation: Lowering the temperature to room temperature resulted in a somewhat lower yield (78%) while selectivities were unaffected.

evidence.text_span: Lowering the temperature or using Pd((R)-SEGPHOS)(H2O)2(OTf)2 instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2, somewhat lower yields were observed; however, the selectivities unaffected (entries 3, 4).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 as the Pd catalyst.
thought.gap: The effect of using a different chiral Pd catalyst on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, Pd((R)-SEGPHOS)(H2O)2(OTf)2 was used instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2.

outcome.observation: Using Pd((R)-SEGPHOS)(H2O)2(OTf)2 resulted in a somewhat lower yield (82%) while selectivities were unaffected.

evidence.text_span: Lowering the temperature or using Pd((R)-SEGPHOS)(H2O)2(OTf)2 instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2, somewhat lower yields were observed; however, the selectivities unaffected (entries 3, 4).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 as the Pd catalyst.
thought.gap: The effect of using other chiral metal catalysts on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, Ni((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 was used instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2.

outcome.observation: Ni((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 showed little or no diastereoselectivity (1:1 dr) and inferior yield (54%) and enantioselectivity (71%, 64%).

evidence.text_span: Other chiral catalysts, for example, Ni((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2, Cu((S,S)-t-butyl-box)Γ(H2O)2(OTf)2, and the complexes consisting of Cu(II)(OTf)2 or Cu(I)(CH3CN)4BF4 and (S,Sp)-iPr-Phosferrox show little or no diastereoselectivity and the observed yield or enantioselectivity is inferior to that obtained for Pd-ligand complexes (entries 5-8).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 as the Pd catalyst.
thought.gap: The effect of using other chiral metal catalysts on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, Cu((S,S)-t-butyl-box)(H2O)2(OTf)2 was used instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2.

outcome.observation: Cu((S,S)-t-butyl-box)(H2O)2(OTf)2 showed low diastereoselectivity (2.5:1 dr) and inferior yield (65%) and enantioselectivity (84%, 55%).

evidence.text_span: Other chiral catalysts, for example, Ni((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2, Cu((S,S)-t-butyl-box)Γ(H2O)2(OTf)2, and the complexes consisting of Cu(II)(OTf)2 or Cu(I)(CH3CN)4BF4 and (S,Sp)-iPr-Phosferrox show little or no diastereoselectivity and the observed yield or enantioselectivity is inferior to that obtained for Pd-ligand complexes (entries 5-8).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 as the Pd catalyst.
thought.gap: The effect of using other chiral metal catalysts on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, Cu(OTf)2 and (S,Sp)-L were used instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2.

outcome.observation: Cu(OTf)2 and (S,Sp)-L showed low diastereoselectivity (3.5:1 dr) and inferior yield (75%) and enantioselectivity (88%, 85%).

evidence.text_span: Other chiral catalysts, for example, Ni((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2, Cu((S,S)-t-butyl-box)Γ(H2O)2(OTf)2, and the complexes consisting of Cu(II)(OTf)2 or Cu(I)(CH3CN)4BF4 and (S,Sp)-iPr-Phosferrox show little or no diastereoselectivity and the observed yield or enantioselectivity is inferior to that obtained for Pd-ligand complexes (entries 5-8).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 as the Pd catalyst.
thought.gap: The effect of using other chiral metal catalysts on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, Cu(CH3CN)4BF4 and (S,Sp)-L were used instead of Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2.

outcome.observation: Cu(CH3CN)4BF4 and (S,Sp)-L showed low diastereoselectivity (3.5:1 dr) and inferior yield (49%) and enantioselectivity (98%, 84%).

evidence.text_span: Other chiral catalysts, for example, Ni((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2, Cu((S,S)-t-butyl-box)Γ(H2O)2(OTf)2, and the complexes consisting of Cu(II)(OTf)2 or Cu(I)(CH3CN)4BF4 and (S,Sp)-iPr-Phosferrox show little or no diastereoselectivity and the observed yield or enantioselectivity is inferior to that obtained for Pd-ligand complexes (entries 5-8).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using Pd((R)-BINAP)(H2O)2(OTf)2 as the Pd catalyst.
thought.gap: The effect of using a different metal salt on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, Zn(OTf)2 was used instead of the chiral Pd complex.

outcome.observation: Employing Zn(OTf)2 instead of the chiral Pd complex provided decreased yield (58%) and similarly poor diastereoselectivity (2.5:1 dr), though ee values were still good (92%, 75%).

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, employing Zn(II)(OTf)2 instead of chiral Pd complex or using a combination of racemic Ir complex along with chiral Pd catalyst, both cases provided decreased yield and similarly poor diastereoselectivity; however, the ee values are still good (entries 9, 10).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation were established using a chiral Ir ligand (S)-L.
thought.gap: The effect of using a racemic Ir complex on the reaction yield and selectivity needed to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate the catalyst effect, a racemic Ir complex ((rac)-L) was used along with the chiral Pd catalyst.

outcome.observation: Using a combination of racemic Ir complex along with chiral Pd catalyst provided decreased yield (70%) and similarly poor diastereoselectivity (2:1 dr), though ee values were still good (92%, -91%).

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, employing Zn(II)(OTf)2 instead of chiral Pd complex or using a combination of racemic Ir complex along with chiral Pd catalyst, both cases provided decreased yield and similarly poor diastereoselectivity; however, the ee values are still good (entries 9, 10).
evidence.section: Results and discussion

thought.background: The Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation methodology was optimized for model substrates.
thought.gap: The application of this methodology to the more complex pyrrole allylic alcohol 13, a key intermediate for parvistemoline alkaloids, needed to be demonstrated. The functional complexity of 13 might require adjustments to the optimized conditions.
thought.decision: To apply the optimized Ir/Pd-dual-catalyzed allylic alkylation to pyrrole allylic alcohol 13 and tert-butyl 3-oxobutanoate (11), with adjustments to catalyst loading and temperature to account for increased functional complexity.

outcome.observation: Keto ester 14 was obtained in 79% yield with high levels of diastereo- and enantioselectivity by doubling the catalyst loading and decreasing the temperature to -20 °C.

evidence.text_span: Gratifyingly, doubling the catalyst loading and decreasing the temperature to −20 °C could deliver the keto ester 14 in 79% yield with high levels of diastereo- and enantioselectivity.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Keto ester 14 was successfully synthesized with high stereoselectivity.
thought.gap: The ketone group of 14 needed to be reduced diastereoselectively to install another stereocenter.
thought.decision: To reduce the ketone group of 14 using Zn(BH4)2, expecting high diastereoselectivity via the Zimmerman-Traxler model.

outcome.observation: Erythro-3-hydroxy-2-substituted alcohol 15 was furnished diastereoselectively in 92% yield. The high diastereoselectivity was attributed to the highly coordinating ability of Zn to carbonyl oxygens via the Zimmerman-Traxler model.

evidence.text_span: Reducing the ketone group of 14 using Zn(BH4)2 furnished erythro-3-hydroxy-2-substituted alcohol 15 diastereoselectively in 92% yield. The high diastereoselectivity was attributed to the highly coordinating ability of Zn to carbonyl oxygens via Zimmerman-Traxler model.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Keto ester 14 was successfully synthesized, and the ketone group could be reduced diastereoselectively using Zn(BH4)2 to give erythro-3-hydroxy-2-substituted alcohol 15.
thought.gap: To access different stereoisomers of 15, an alternative reducing agent that would lead to the threo-diastereomer was needed.
thought.decision: To attempt the reduction of 14 using K-selectride, expecting to produce the threo-3-hydroxyl diastereomer of 15 with high selectivity through the Felkin-Ahn model.

outcome.observation: The threo-3-hydroxyl diastereomer of 15 would be produced in high selectivity through the Felkin-Ahn model, but this was not the desired erythro-isomer for the main synthesis line. This was a deliberate choice to access other stereoisomers, not a failure in the sense of an unwanted outcome.

evidence.text_span: Notably, if reduced by K-selectride, threo-3-hydroxyl diastereomer of 15 would be produced in high selectivity through the Felkin-Ahn model.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Intermediate 15, containing the necessary stereocenters, was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: The seven-membered ring needed to be closed to form the tetracyclic framework.
thought.decision: To employ a Ring-Closing Metathesis (RCM) reaction using Grubbs’ second-generation catalyst to close the seven-membered ring of 15.

outcome.observation: RCM reaction of 15 employing Grubbs’ second-generation catalyst delivered 16 in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: To close the seven-membered ring, an RCM reaction of 15 employing Grubbs’ second-generation catalyst delivered 16 in 88% yield (Fig. 7).
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Intermediate 16 was obtained after RCM.
thought.gap: The hydroxyl group needed to be protected as a TBS silyl ether, and the tert-butyl ester needed to be cleaved to a carboxylic acid.
thought.decision: To perform a one-pot reaction involving TBSOTf/Et3N for silyl ether formation and TMSOTf for tert-butyl ester cleavage to obtain TBS-protected carboxylic acid 17.

outcome.observation: Transformation of 16 to the TBS-protected carboxylic acid 17 was accomplished in 85% yield by a one-pot reaction.

evidence.text_span: Transformation of 16 to the TBS-protected carboxylic acid 17 was accomplished in 85% yield by a one-pot reaction in which furnishing the corresponding TBS silyl ether by addition of TBSOTf/Et3N and then cleavage of tert-butyl ester by TMSOTf.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 17 was obtained.
thought.gap: The carboxylic acid needed to be reduced to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To reduce the carboxylic acid 17 to aldehyde 18 using a newly reported methodology involving pinacol borane as a reducing agent and triflyl pyridinium as an activating reagent.

outcome.observation: The reduction of the carboxylic acid to aldehyde 18 was achieved in 65% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent reduction of the carboxylic acid to aldehyde has been achieved through a newly reported methodology where pinacol borane is used as a reducing agent and triflyl pyridinium as an activating reagent for carboxylic acid63. The resulting aldehyde 18 was converted to α-exomethylene γ-butyrolactone 20 stereospecifically through an Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation under the conditions developed by Krische where (S)-Ir-tol-BINAP as a catalyst and acrylic ester 19 as an allylic metal surrogate64–68.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Aldehyde 18 was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: The α-exomethylene γ-butyrolactone moiety needed to be installed stereospecifically.
thought.decision: To convert aldehyde 18 to α-exomethylene γ-butyrolactone 20 stereospecifically through an Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation under Krische's conditions, using (S)-Ir-tol-BINAP as a catalyst and acrylic ester 19 as an allylic metal surrogate.

outcome.observation: The chemical yield of 20 was modest (53%) due to incomplete conversion of the substrate 18 and the formation of byproducts.

evidence.text_span: The resulting aldehyde 18 was converted to α-exomethylene γ-butyrolactone 20 stereospecifically through an Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation under the conditions developed by Krische where (S)-Ir-tol-BINAP as a catalyst and acrylic ester 19 as an allylic metal surrogate64–68. However, the chemical yield of 20 is modest due to incomplete conversion of the substrate 18 and the formation of byproducts
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: The conversion of aldehyde 18 to α-exomethylene γ-butyrolactone 20 using (S)-Ir-tol-BINAP resulted in a modest yield.
thought.gap: The authors noted a potential mismatched effect with the chiral aldehyde 18 and the (S)-Ir-tol-BINAP catalyst, suggesting that the antipodal catalyst might lead to a different outcome.
thought.decision: To attempt the reaction with the antipodal chiral catalyst (R)-Ir-tol-BINAP to see if it would lead to epi-20 selectively and potentially with better yield or selectivity.

outcome.observation: The same reaction of 18 with antipodal chiral catalyst (R)-Ir-tol-BINAP led to epi-20 selectively in 80% yield. This was not a failure in terms of yield, but rather a deliberate exploration of stereodivergence, leading to a different stereoisomer.

evidence.text_span: in contrast, the same reaction of 18 with antipodal chiral catalyst (R)-Ir-to-BINAP led to epi-20 selectively in 80% yield (the difference in reactivity of two Krische catalysts shows a potential mismatched effect on (S)-Ir-tol-BINAP with chiral aldehyde 18, see S41 and S42 of Supplementary Information).
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: The α-exomethylene γ-butyrolactone 20 was synthesized.
thought.gap: The exo-methylene group needed to be isomerized to an endocyclic double bond, and the TBS protecting group needed to be removed.
thought.decision: To perform a ruthenium-catalyzed isomerization of the exo-methylene of 20 to an endocyclic double bond, followed by addition of hydrochloric acid to remove the TBS protecting group, to obtain alcohol 21 in a one-pot reaction.

outcome.observation: Ruthenium-catalyzed isomerization of the exo-methylene of 20 to an endocyclic double bond afforded a 3-methyl furanone intermediate, which was then treated with hydrochloric acid to remove the TBS protecting group, providing alcohol 21 in 85% yield in a one-pot reaction.

evidence.text_span: Ruthenium-catalyzed isomerization of the exo-methylene of 20 to an endocyclic double bond afforded 3-methyl furanone intermediate (not shown), to which was added hydrochloric acid to remove TBS protecting group, providing alcohol 21 in 85% yield in a one-pot reaction.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Alcohol 21 was obtained.
thought.gap: The fused tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold needed to be formed via intramolecular cyclization.
thought.decision: To treat 21 with DBU to promote an intramolecular oxy-Michael addition, expecting to form the fused tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold.

outcome.observation: Treatment of 21 with DBU resulted in a smooth intramolecular oxy-Michael addition, giving a 3:1 mixture in 90% combined yield, favoring the desired 22a. The minor diastereomer 22b was a crystalline solid, allowing for X-ray crystallographic analysis to confirm the stereochemistry.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of 21 with DBU, an intramolecular oxy-Michael addition proceeded smoothly to give a 3:1 mixture in 90% combined yield, favoring the desired 22a. The minor diastereomer 22b, differentiating in configuration at α-methyl group of lactone, proved to be a crystalline solid, and thus, single crystal X-ray analysis was employed to unambiguously assign the illustrated stereochemistry of the newly formed fused tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone scaffold (CCDC 2394438).
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: The tetracyclic framework 22a was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: The olefin in the seven-membered ring needed to be hydrogenated.
thought.decision: To hydrogenate the olefin in the seven-membered ring of 22a using a modified procedure with a 1:1 mixture of Pd/C and Pearlman’s catalyst under hydrogen atmosphere.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation of the olefin in the seven-membered ring of 22a using a 1:1 mixture of Pd/C and Pearlman’s catalyst gave 23 in 95% yield.

evidence.text_span: With tetracycle 22a in hand, as shown in Fig. 9, we continued our total synthesis by hydrogenating the olefin in the seven-membered ring. The modified procedure using a 1:1 mixture of Pd/C and Pearlman’s catalyst gave 23 better results than the conventional method using a single catalyst.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Intermediate 23 was obtained after hydrogenation.
thought.gap: The lactone and methyl ester groups needed to be reduced to a lactol and primary alcohol, respectively, and then oxidized to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To perform a global reduction of the lactone and methyl ester of 23 with DIBAL-H, followed by oxidation with the Ley-Griffith protocol, to furnish aldehyde 24.

outcome.observation: Subsequent global reduction of the lactone and methyl ester of 23 with DIBAL-H furnished corresponding lactol and primary alcohol, respectively, which was oxidized with the Ley-Griffith protocol and gave aldehyde 24 in good yield. 24 is a crystalline compound whose structure was confirmed by X-ray crystallography (CCDC 2394439).

evidence.text_span: Subsequent global reduction of the lactone and methyl ester of 23 with DIBAL-H furnished corresponding lactol and primary alcohol, respectively, which was oxidized with the Ley-Griffith protocol and gave aldehyde 24 in good yield. 24 is a crystalline compound whose structure was confirmed by X-ray crystallography (CCDC 2394439).
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Pyrrole-substituted aldehyde 24 was obtained.
thought.gap: The aldehyde group needed to be removed to obtain the pyrrole 25.
thought.decision: To use rhodium-catalyzed decarbonylation of aldehyde conditions to convert 24 to pyrrole 25.

outcome.observation: Pyrrole 25 was obtained in 78% yield using rhodium-catalyzed decarbonylation of aldehyde conditions.

evidence.text_span: With pyrrole-substituted aldehyde 24 in hand, using rhodium-catalyzed decarbonylation of aldehyde conditions, pyrrole 25 could be obtained in 78% yield.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Pyrrole 25 was obtained.
thought.gap: The pyrrole moiety needed to be oxidized to a dihydro-2H-pyrrol-2-one intermediate and then reduced to parvistemoline (1).
thought.decision: To oxidize pyrrole 25 using freshly purified m-CPBA under Dai’s conditions to give the dihydro-2H-pyrrol-2-one intermediate, followed by Pd/C catalyzed hydrogenation to smoothly provide parvistemoline (1).

outcome.observation: Pyrrole 25 was oxidized using freshly purified m-CPBA to give the dihydro-2H-pyrrol-2-one intermediate, which was further reduced with Pd/C catalyzed hydrogenation, smoothly providing parvistemoline (1). The synthetic product is spectroscopically and analytically identical to those of the natural source.

evidence.text_span: Thus obtained pyrrole 25 was oxidized using freshly purified m-CPBA under Dai’s conditions23 to give the dihydro-2H-pyrrol-2-one intermediate, which was further reduced with Pd/C catalyzed hydrogenation, smoothly providing parvistemoline (1). As anticipated, our synthetic product is spectroscopically and analytically identical to those of the natural source.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Aldehyde 24 was obtained.
thought.gap: The α-exo-methylene γ-butyrolactone attached to the pyrrole core needed to be installed to synthesize didehydroparvistemonine (3).
thought.decision: To use Krische’s Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation of aldehyde 24 to install the α-exo-methylene γ-butyrolactone, expecting to obtain 26.

outcome.observation: Krische’s Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation of aldehyde 24 afforded 26 in 3:1 ratio favoring C18 (S) isomer in 87% combined yield. 26 is unstable and used for the next step as soon as possible.

evidence.text_span: To this end, as shown in Fig. 9, installing the α-exo-methylene γ-butyrolactone attached to the pyrrole core using Krische’s Ir-catalyzed 2-(alkoxycarbonyl)allylation of aldehyde 24 afforded 26 in 3:1 ratio favoring C18 (S) isomer in 87% combined yield. 26 is unstable and used for the next step as soon as possible.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Intermediate 26 was obtained, but it was unstable.
thought.gap: The final step to didehydroparvistemonine (3) involved hydrogenation.
thought.decision: To use Takaya’s asymmetric hydrogenation conditions to deliver didehydroparvistemonine (3) along with its C18 (R) isomer.

outcome.observation: Takaya’s asymmetric hydrogenation conditions were used to deliver didehydroparvistemonine (3) along with its C18 (R) isomer in quantitative combined yield. The synthetic sample of 3 was purified with HPLC, whose NMR data are in full agreement with those of the isolation paper; however, the magnitude of optical rotation exhibits a noticeable difference despite the identical sign. Didehydroparvistemonine (3) proved to be a crystalline compound, and a single-crystal X-ray analysis was employed to assign absolute stereochemistry (CCDC 2286427).

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, Takaya’s asymmetric hydrogenation conditions69 were used to deliver didehydroparvistemonine (3) along with its C18 (R) isomer in quantitative combined yield. The synthetic sample of 3 was purified with HPLC, whose NMR data are in full agreement with those of the isolation paper; however, the magnitude of optical rotation exhibits a noticeable difference despite the identical sign. This difference could be explained by the following two factors. First, we found that a didehydroparvistemonine sample could not dissolve well in methanol but was completely dissolved in CHCl3. Consequently, we measured the optical rotation in CHCl3. Second, as we elucidated previously, a partial epimerization in solution occurs at C18 attached to the pyrrole core, which was usually unnoticed by isolation authors before we recently rationalized during the total synthesis47. Fortunately, didehydroparvistemonine (3) proved to be a crystalline compound, and then a single-crystal X-ray analysis was employed to assign absolute stereochemistry (CCDC 2286427).
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Parvistemoline (1) was in hand.
thought.gap: The synthesis of parvistemonine (2) required a vinylogous Mannich reaction to form the pentacyclic structure.
thought.decision: To treat parvistemoline (1) with Vaska complex and tetramethyldisiloxane (TMDS) to generate an enamine intermediate, followed by one-pot addition of 2-siloxyfuran 27 and 2-nitrobenzoic acid, to obtain pentacyclic 28 and epi-28.

outcome.observation: Treatment of parvistemoline (1) with Vaska complex and TMDS generated an enamine intermediate, subsequent one-pot addition of 2-siloxyfuran 27 and 2-nitrobenzoic acid provided pentacyclic 28 and epi-28, which were separated by flash chromatography on silica gel, in 36% combined yield.

evidence.text_span: With parvistemoline (1) in hand, inspired by Chida-Sato’s synthesis of stemonine19,24, an iridium-catalyzed hydrosilylation was used. As shown in Fig. 9, treatment of parvistemoline (1) with Vaska complex [IrCl(CO)(PPh3)2] and tetramethyldisiloxane (TMDS) generated an enamine intermediate (not shown), subsequent one-pot addition of 2-siloxyfuran 27 and 2-nitrobenzoic acid provided pentacyclic 28 and epi-28, which were separated by flash chromatography on silica gel, in 36% combined yield.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Pentacyclic 28 and epi-28 were obtained, but the yield was modest, and the epi-isomer was present.
thought.gap: The authors considered isomerizing epi-28 to 28 to improve the overall yield of the desired isomer.
thought.decision: To attempt treatment of epi-28 with DBU to isomerize it to 28.

outcome.observation: Treatment of epi-28 with DBU failed to effectively isomerize to 28 mainly because a malign epimerization occurred at the site of tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone.

evidence.text_span: Notably, treatment of epi-28 with DBU failed to effectively isomerize to 28 mainly because a malign epimerization occurred at the site of tetrahydrofuran-γ-lactone.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: The previous vinylogous Mannich reaction yielded 28 and epi-28 in modest yield.
thought.gap: An alternative, more efficient approach was needed to synthesize parvistemonine (2).
thought.decision: To pursue an alternative approach: hydrolysis of the methyl ester of 29a with 48% hydrobromic acid, followed by treatment with oxalyl chloride in the presence of DMF to deliver acid chloride intermediate 30, which decomposes into an iminium salt. Then, 2-siloxyfuran 27 would be added to the resultant iminium.

outcome.observation: Hydrolysis of methyl ester of 29a followed by treatment with oxalyl chloride and addition of 2-siloxyfuran 27 resulted in much better results, providing pentacyclic 28 and epi-28 in 68% overall yield.

evidence.text_span: Considering the modest yield on the vinylogous Mannich reaction employing iridium-catalyzed hydrosilylation, we pursued an alternative approach at this junction. Hydrolysis of methyl ester of 29a with 48% hydrobromic acid followed by treatment with oxalyl chloride in the presence of DMF delivered acid chloride intermediate 30, which decomposes into the iminium salt8,70. Analogously, to the resultant iminium was added 2-siloxyfuran 27, and much better results occurred, providing pentacyclic 28 and epi-28 in 68% overall yield.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Pentacyclic 28 was obtained.
thought.gap: The final step to parvistemonine (2) involved stereoselective hydrogenation.
thought.decision: To perform stereoselective hydrogenation of 28 using Rh/Al2O3 with 5 bar of hydrogen gas to yield parvistemonine (2).

outcome.observation: Stereoselective hydrogenation of 28 quantitatively yielded parvistemonine (2). Its spectral properties fully matched the natural isolate.

evidence.text_span: Finally, using Rh/Al2O3 with 5 bar of hydrogen gas19, stereoselective hydrogenation of 28 quantitively yielded parvistemonine (2). Its spectral properties fully matched the natural isolate.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

thought.background: Parvistemonine (2) was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: To further solidify the conclusion on the synthesis of the title alkaloids, parvistemonine (2) could be converted to didehydroparvistemonine (3), whose structure was unambiguously established by X-ray crystallography.
thought.decision: To convert parvistemonine (2) into didehydroparvistemonine (3) through MnO2 oxidation.

outcome.observation: Parvistemonine (2) was converted into didehydroparvistemonine (3) through MnO2 oxidation. This oxidation further solidifies the conclusion on the synthesis of title alkaloids.

evidence.text_span: Additionally, we converted this material into didehydroparvistemonine (3) through MnO2 oxidation24. Because the latter’s chemical structure was unambiguously established by X-ray crystallography, this oxidation further solidifies our conclusion on the synthesis of title alkaloids.
evidence.section: Asymmetric total synthesis of parvistemoline alkaloids

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated for the entire synthesis, but individual steps show good to excellent yields.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The total synthesis of parvistemoline (1) and didehydroparvistemonine (3) was achieved in 13-14 steps.

Total Steps.interpretation: The total synthesis of parvistemoline (1) and didehydroparvistemonine (3) was achieved in 13-14 steps.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Several conditions and catalysts were screened, leading to lower yields or selectivities, which were documented as failed attempts in the context of optimization.

validation.significance: The total syntheses of parvistemoline (1), parvistemonine (2), and didehydroparvistemonine (3) were accomplished in 13-14 steps. A key highlight is the development and deployment of an Ir/Pd-synergistically-catalyzed allylation, which successfully controls the absolute stereochemistry of two tertiary centers, one of which is prone to racemization. This methodology, combined with other stereoselective reactions, allowed for the installation of multiple contiguous tertiary stereocenters. The strategies developed are expected to be applicable to other complex natural products.

source.title: Total syntheses of the parvistemoline alkaloids enabled by stereocontrolled Ir/Pd-catalyzed allylic alkylation.
source.authors: Liang X, Ding QH, Yang JT, Yang HF, Deng Y, Shi L, Wei K, Yang YR.
